title-group.article-title: Association between oral microbial shifts and polycystic ovary syndrome: a systematic review

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Oral Medicine, Stomatology Hospital, School of Stomatology, Zhejiang University School of Medicine, Zhejiang Provincial Clinical Research Center for Oral Diseases, Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Oral Biomedical, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China

bDepartment of Oral Medicine, Peking University School and Hospital of Stomatology & National Center for Stomatology & National Clinical Research Center for Oral Diseases & National Engineering Research Center of Oral Biomaterials and Digital Medical Devices, Beijing, China

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Zhimin Yan yzhimin96@163.com Department of Oral Medicine, Peking University School and Hospital of Stomatology & National Center for Stomatology & National Clinical Research Center for Oral Diseases & National Engineering Research Center of Oral Biomaterials and Digital Medical Devices, No.22, Zhongguancun South Avenue, Haidian District, Beijing, 100081, China

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: This systematic review aimed to assess the association between the oral microbiota and polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and elucidate its potential clinical significance.

sec.title: Materials and methods
sec.p: A comprehensive search of eight databases and other sources from inception to 13 January 2026, identified 10 eligible studies from 310 records, involving 334 PCOS patients and analyzed oral microbiota species and their clinical parameters correlation.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Oral microbiota alterations in PCOS women showed consistent and heterogeneous features, with Fusobacterium elevation as the most robust finding. However, the results for Porphyromonas gingivalis and Fusobacterium nucleatum varied with periodontal status. Specific oral microbial changes are correlated with core systemic features of PCOS, including hormonal dysregulation (e.g. elevated testosterone and luteinizing hormone) and metabolic disturbances (e.g. dyslipidemia and insulin resistance).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Oral microbiota alterations in PCOS are characterized by consistent Fusobacterium elevation and heterogeneous changes associated with systemic inflammation, metabolic‒endocrine disturbances and poor periodontal health. Despite the limited current evidence, the oral microbiota may serve as a novel entry point for PCOS management. Large, high‑quality prospective studies are warranted to clarify the causal relationship between the oral microbiota and PCOS, and to explore the feasibility of modulating the oral microbiota as a novel therapeutic strategy.

list-item.p: Consistent alteration in the oral microbiota of women with PCOS is the elevated abundance of Fusobacterium.
Levels of Porphyromonas gingivalis and Fusobacterium nucleatum in women with PCOS vary with periodontal status.
Oral microbial changes in women with PCOS are associated with metabolic and endocrine disturbances.

institution-wrap.institution: Zhejiang Provincial Science and Technology Program for Disease Control and Prevention
Joint TCM Science & Technology Projects of National Demonstration Zones for Comprehensive TCM Reform

funding-group.funding-statement: This work was supported by the [Joint TCM Science & Technology Projects of National Demonstration Zones for Comprehensive TCM Reform] under Grant [number GZY-KJS-ZJ-2026-073]; and [Zhejiang Provincial Science and Technology Program for Disease Control and Prevention] under Grant [number 2026JKZ044].

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The oral microbiota constitutes an essential component of the human microbiome, colonizing the dynamic oral microenvironment [1]. Evidence indicates that alterations in the oral microbiota are closely associated with a variety of diseases. These include not only local oral pathologies such as periodontitis but also systemic disorders, including rheumatoid arthritis, gastrointestinal diseases and endocrine conditions [2–4]. Furthermore, hormonal fluctuations due to puberty [5], pregnancy [6] and oral contraceptives [7] have been reported to influence the oral microbiota.

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a prevalent endocrine disorder affecting women of reproductive age. It is also recognized as a metabolic syndrome associated with several risk factors, including cardiovascular disease, insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, endothelial dysfunction and visceral obesity. Despite its relatively high incidence, the etiology of PCOS remains unclear and may be related to genetic, environmental, obesity-related and other factors [8]. In recent years, with advances in microbiome research, the role of the microbiota in the pathogenesis of PCOS has gradually attracted widespread attention. The oral microbiota has emerged as a critical research subject due to its distinctive properties, with key reasons including: First, the existence of the oral‒gut axis suggests that oral microbial disturbances can influence systemic health [9], including potential pathways relevant to PCOS. The correlation between the gut microbiota and PCOS has been substantiated by numerous clinical and experimental studies [10–12], lending biological plausibility to the oral microbiota as a potential modulator. Second, the oral microbiota is in direct and continuous interaction with the host immune system and is highly sensitive to endocrine fluctuations [13,14], both central features of PCOS pathophysiology. Third, compared to gut samples, oral samples are non-invasive and easier to collect, facilitating clinical translation. Given these premises, initial research has begun to explore the oral microbiota-PCOS link. Yet, evidence remains scarce, fragmented and predominantly observational. The specific compositional shifts of the oral microbiota in PCOS, their consistency across populations, and their mechanistic contributions to the syndrome's features are poorly understood.

Therefore, this systematic review aims to synthesize and appraise existing observational evidence on oral microbiota alterations in women with PCOS compared with controls, to identify methodological heterogeneity and its potential impact on findings, and to highlight knowledge gaps while proposing directions for future research on causality, biomarkers and translation.

sec.title: Study design and protocol
sec.p:
The present systematic review followed the PRISMA 2020 guidelines (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) [15]. This systematic review was prospectively registered in the PROSPERO database with the registration number: CRD420251272267. To clarify the core research question and ensure the rigor, transparency and reproducibility of the study design, we explicitly define the PICO framework that guided this systematic review.•
Population: Reproductive-aged women diagnosed with PCOS (Rotterdam criteria) [16], compared with healthy controls matched for key baseline characteristics.•
Intervention/exposure: Alterations in oral microbiota composition, including changes in the relative abundance of specific bacterial taxa, and fluctuations in alpha/beta diversity indices.•
Comparison: The oral microbiota profile in healthy, reproductive-aged women without PCOS or other systemic diseases, matched for key baseline characteristics.•
Outcome:
Primary outcomes: Quantitative and qualitative differences in oral microbiota profiles.

Secondary outcomes: Associations between PCOS and key parameters, including periodontal inflammatory status, systemic humoral immune responses to oral pathogens, hormonal profiles, glucose/lipid metabolic markers and their correlations with oral microbiome characteristics.

sec.title: Database sources and search strategy
sec.p: A comprehensive literature search was performed across multiple electronic databases and sources to identify comparative studies exploring the associations between oral microbiota and polycystic ovary syndrome, published in either English or Chinese. The following databases were systematically searched: Embase, PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, the Cochrane Library, China National Knowledge Infrastructure (CNKI), VIP China Science and Technology Journal Database and Wanfang Database. Additionally, to ensure thorough coverage of both published and unpublished research, the search was extended to include ClinicalTrials.gov and ProQuest Dissertations & Theses Global (PQDT). The search covered records from the inception of each bibliographic database up to 13 January 2026. Searches in ClinicalTrials.gov and PQDT also aimed to identify all relevant records from their earliest available entries, with no restrictions on publication or registration year. The search strategy employed the following key terms: (‘polycystic ovarian syndrome’ OR ‘PCOS’ OR ‘Stein-Leventhal syndrome’) AND (‘oral microbiota’ OR ‘oral microbiome’ OR ‘oral flora’ OR ‘mouth flora’ OR ‘saliva microbiome’ OR ‘subgingival plaque microbiome’), and search syntaxes were tailored to each database as needed, with appropriate use of MeSH terms (for PubMed) and Emtree terms (for Embase) to enhance comprehensiveness. In addition to electronic searches, the reference lists of the included studies were manually screened to identify potentially relevant publications. This search was conducted by two independent researchers (QM and CL).

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: The inclusion criteria were strictly defined as follows: (1) Study design: original human comparative studies (prospective or retrospective case‒control, cohort or cross-sectional) with a clearly matched healthy control group. (2) Participants: reproductive-aged women diagnosed with PCOS according to the Rotterdam criteria [16]. Healthy female controls were required to be matched to the case group in terms of baseline characteristics, with no history of PCOS, with no antibiotic or probiotic administration within the recent 3 months. (3) Outcome measures: the primary outcomes were defined as data on the oral microbiome from saliva or subgingival plaque, including microbial community structure, species abundance and α/β diversity, as well as quantitative detection of specific pathogenic bacteria. Secondary outcomes included PCOS-related clinical indicators, inflammatory markers and periodontal health indicators. (4) Publication type and language: peer-reviewed original research articles published in either English or Chinese, with no restrictions on publication date.

The exclusion criteria were as follows: (1) Unavailable full text or published only as an abstract. (2) Non-original research, including editorials, letters, case reports, case series, reviews, meta-analyses and animal or in vitro experimental studies. (3) Absence of a healthy control group or inadequate matching of baseline characteristics between groups. (4) Lack of extractable data on the association between the oral microbiota and PCOS.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: The selection of studies was processed according to PRISMA guidelines. After initial database retrieval, duplicate records were removed using EndNote reference management software, and the articles were then screened independently by two reviewers (QM and CL) based on titles and abstracts against the predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria, followed by full-text assessment of potentially eligible studies for final eligibility. Any discrepancies during screening were resolved through consensus discussion, or by consulting a third senior reviewer (ZY) if no agreement was reached.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Two reviewers (QM and CL) independently extracted full-text data from the included studies using a predefined standardized data extraction form. This form was designed in accordance with the objectives of the present study and the PRISMA guidelines, with core items covering study characteristic variables and outcome variables (Table S1). The consistency of data extraction was assessed using Cohen's kappa coefficient, with a resultant kappa value of 0.86 indicating a high level of inter-reviewer consistency (≥0.75 denotes substantial agreement). Any discrepancies arising during the extraction process were first addressed by cross-checking the contentious items and conducting discussions based on the original literature, if no consensus was reached through consultation, a third senior reviewer (ZY) was consulted to make the final decision.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p: Two independent reviewers (QM and CL) assessed the methodological quality of the included studies using the Newcastle‒Ottawa Scale (NOS) [17]. The NOS evaluates studies across three domains including selection (maximum 4 stars), comparability (maximum 2 stars) and outcome (maximum 3 stars), yielding a total score of 0–9 stars. Based on the total score, studies were categorized as having high (≥7 stars), moderate (5–6 stars) or low (≤4 stars) overall methodological quality. Furthermore, the risk of bias for each domain (selection, comparability and outcome) was judged as low, moderate or high according to the criteria and stars awarded in the respective NOS items. Any discrepancies between the two reviewers (QM and CL) during the NOS assessment were resolved through consultation with a third senior investigator (ZY) to ensure consistency in the quality and bias evaluations.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: Of the 310 records initially identified, 96 duplicates were removed, leaving 214 records for title and abstract screening. Of these, 184 records were excluded for failing to meet the predefined eligibility criteria. Subsequently, 29 articles proceeded to full-text review. Of these, 19 were excluded for the following reasons: ineligible study design, lack of a healthy control group and insufficient extractable data regarding the oral microbiota and PCOS. Ultimately, 10 studies that met all eligibility criteria were included in this systematic review. The PRISMA flow diagram of study selection is depicted in Figure 1.

fig.label: Figure 1.

caption.p: PRISMA flow diagram of study selection.

fig.alt-text: A PRISMA flow diagram illustrates study selection, detailing identification, screening, and inclusion phases.
fig.long-desc: The PRISMA flow diagram illustrates the study selection process, organized into three main phases: Identification, Screening, and Included, vertically aligned on the left. The Identification phase begins with a box labeled "Identification of studies via databases and other methods" at the top center. Below this, a box lists "Records identified from databases and other methods (N equals 310)", with a breakdown by database: PubMed (n equals 34), Embase (n equals 16), Scopus (n equals 60), Web of Science (n equals 48), Cochrane Library (n equals 0), China National Knowledge Infrastructure (n equals 20), Wan fang Database (n equals 59), China Science and Technology Journal Database (n equals 31), ClinicalTrials.g o v (n equals 1), and ProQuest Dissertations and Theses Global (n equals 41). To the right of this, a box details "Records removed before screening", including "Duplicates records removed automatically (n equals 61)", "Duplicates records removed manually (n equals 35)", "Records marked as ineligible by automation tools (n equals 0)", and "Records removed for other reasons (n equals 0)". A downward arrow connects the initial identification box to the next stage. The Screening phase starts with a box labeled "Records screened n equals 214". An arrow points from this box to a right-side box, "Records excluded by reading title slash abstract (n equals 184)".A downward arrow leads to the next box labeled "Reports sought for retrieval (n equals 30)". To the right of this box, another box states "Reports not retrieved (n equals 1)". A downward arrow connects the retrieval-seeking box to "Reports assessed for eligibility (n equals 29)". To the right of this eligibility assessment box, a box lists "Full-text articles excluded (n equals 19)" with four specific reasons: "Ineligible study design (case report) (n equals 1)", "Oral microbiota not reported (n equals 14)", "Ineligible population (n equals 2)", and "No control group (n equals 2)". Finally, a downward arrow connects the eligibility assessment box to the bottom-most box labeled "Total studies included in review (n equals 10)", which represents the Included phase of the study selection process.

sec.title: Basic characteristics of included studies
sec.p: This systematic review included 10 case‒control studies [18–27] conducted across five countries. All studies diagnosed PCOS according to the 2003 Rotterdam criteria [16]. Regarding the study population, a total of 334 PCOS patients and 236 healthy controls were enrolled across the 10 included studies. All studies adjusted for key confounding factors, such as age, body mass index (BMI) and smoking status, with some studies additionally adjusting for dietary habits, oral hygiene practices and the menstrual cycle phase. Oral samples were collected from two niches: saliva (eight studies) and subgingival plaque (two studies). For microbiota analysis, the techniques employed primarily fell into two categories: high-throughput amplicon sequencing (six studies) and quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) (four studies). Detailed methodological characteristics are summarized in Table 1.

caption.p: Basic characteristics of the included studies on oral microbiota and PCOS.

tr.td: 45 PCOS with healthy periodontium35 PCOS with gingivitis(Rotterdam criteria)
25 Systemically and periodontally healthy women20 Healthy women with gingivitis
25 Hormonally healthy(3 hyperandrogenemia, excluded)(2 smoke, excluded)
10 PCOS with healthy periodontium10 PCOS with gingivitis10 PCOS with periodontitis(Rotterdam criteria)
25 PCOS with insulin resistance25 PCOS without insulin resistance(Rotterdam criteria)
25 Healthy with insulin resistance25 Healthy without insulin resistance
Species-specific primersUniversal bacterial primers(specific sequences not published)
9 periodontal pathogensb total subgingival bacterial count
P. gingivalisF: AGGCAGCTTGCCATACTGCGR: ACTGTTAGCAACTACCGATGTF. nucleatumF: GAAGAAACAAATGACGGTAACAACR: GTCATCCCCACCTTCCTCCT

fn.p: Note: All included studies adopted the 2003 Rotterdam diagnostic criteria [16] for polycystic ovary syndrome.

Seven target periodontal pathogens, including Porphyromonas gingivalis (P. gingivalis), Treponema denticola (T. denticola), Tannerella forsythia (T. forsythia), Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans (A. actinomycetemcomitans), Prevotella intermedia (P. intermedia), Fusobacterium nucleatum (F. nucleatum) and S. oralis.

Nine periodontal pathogens include A. actinomycetemcomitans, P. gingivalis, T. forsythia, T. denticola, F. nucleatum, P. intermedia, Peptostreptococcus micros (P. micros), Eubacterium nodatum (E. nodatum), and Capnocytophaga gingivalis (C. gingivalis).

sec.title: Primary outcomes: oral microbiota alterations in PCOS
sec.p: Comparative analysis revealed consistent and heterogeneous differences in the oral microbiota between women with PCOS and controls (Table 2).

caption.p: Oral microbiota alteration data of PCOS patients vs. controls in the included studies.

tr.td: PCOS with gingivitis vs. PCOS with healthy periodontiumPCOS with gingivitis vs. Systemically and periodontally healthy womenPCOS with gingivitis vs. Systemically healthy women with gingivitis
PCOS with gingivitis vs. Systemically and periodontally healthy womenPCOS with gingivitis vs. PCOS with healthy periodontium
PCOS with gingivitis vs. PCOS with healthy periodontium
PCOS with gingivitis vs. Systemically and periodontally healthy womenPCOS with gingivitis vs. Systemically healthy women with gingivitis
Control. ZT0 vs PCOS.ZT0Control. ZT18 vs PCOS.ZT18
PCOS. ZT0 vs. Control.ZT0PCOS. ZT6 vs. Control.ZT6PCOS. ZT12 vs. Control.ZT12PCOS. ZT18 vs. Control.ZT18
PCOS. ZT6 vs. PCOS.ZT18Control. ZT6 vs. Control.ZT18
PCOS. ZT6 vs. PCOS.ZT18Control. ZT6 vs. Control.ZT18
Treponema denticolaRed complex bacteriaOrange complex bacteria(infection rate)
A. actinomycetemcomitansPorphyromonas gingivalisTannerella forsythiaFusobacterium nucleatumPrevotella intermediaCapnocytophaga gingivalis(infection rate, counts)
PCOS with periodontitis vs. Heathy control

PCOS with insulin resistance vs. Healthy with insulin resistancePCOS with insulin resistance vs. Healthy without insulin resistancePCOS without insulin resistance vs. Healthy with insulin resistancePCOS without insulin resistance vs. Healthy without insulin resistance

fn.p: Note: Results of the LEfSe (LDA Effect Size) analysis are presented as LDA scores (log10), which estimate the effect size of each differentially abundant feature. Results of other analyses are presented as p values.

Taxonomic names in the table are formatted according to international nomenclature rules: genus names and below (e.g. species) are italicized; names at the rank of family and above (e.g. family, order, class and phylum) are in upright (non-italic) font.

sec.title: Diversity and structural alterations in the oral microbiome
sec.p: The included studies presented heterogeneous findings for alpha and beta diversity in the oral microbiota of women with PCOS compared to healthy controls. For alpha diversity, the results were inconsistent, with one study [19] finding no significant differences, while others reported decreased [20,27] or increased [22,24,26] diversity in PCOS groups. Notably, Li et al. [20] observed a significant decrease in the Shannon and Chao1 indices, specifically at the ZT0 circadian time point. Regarding beta diversity (community structure), the evidence was also mixed. Three studies [20,24,26] demonstrated a significant separation between PCOS and control groups using various distance metrics. In contrast, two studies [22,27] found no statistically significant differences. The findings from Lindheim et al. [19] were nuanced: they reported no significant difference using weighted UniFrac but observed a borderline significant separation (p = 0.05) with the unweighted UniFrac metric. This observed heterogeneity in diversity outcomes is likely attributable to methodological variations, including differences in sample type, targeted 16S rRNA gene regions, sequencing platforms and the specific bioinformatic pipelines employed.

fig.label: Figure 2.

caption.p: The schematic diagram of the possible mechanisms of oral microbiota affecting polycystic ovary syndrome.

fig.alt-text: A 3-panel diagram shows oral microbiota affecting polycystic ovary syndrome, detailing bacterial changes and systemic.
fig.long-desc: The 3-panel diagram shows the effect of oral microbiota on polycystic ovary syndrome, detailing bacterial changes and systemic impacts. The leftmost panel displays a schematic outline of a human body including the oral cavity, digestive system, and reproductive system. An arrow from the polycystic ovaries points to a text box. The upper right panel shows an open mouth, with three associated text boxes. The upper left box indicates Actinobacteria, Firmicutes, and Streptococcus with downward arrows, signifying decreased abundance. The lower left box shows Fusobacterium with an upward arrow, specifically F. nucleatum, indicating increased abundance. The right box shows Proteobacteria with an upward arrow, indicating increased abundance; P. gingivalis has an upward arrow, labeled PCOS with periodontitis below it, indicating increased abundance in this population. The lower right panel presents a magnified cross section of oral tissue, depicting bacteria infiltrating the epithelial layers, with some entering the bloodstream. Insets highlight inflammatory markers: CRP, TNF-alpha, IL-6, and IL-1, all with upward arrows, indicating elevated levels. This panel is linked to the human body via a text box listing hormonal and metabolic markers: increased TT, increased LH, increased LH/FSH ratio, increased TG, and decreased HDL‑C.

sec.title: Differentially abundant taxa in PCOS
sec.p: Alterations in the abundance of specific oral taxa were a prominent finding, with a notable and consistent elevation of bacteria within the genus Fusobacterium being the most robust signal among the altered taxa. At the genus level, Fusobacterium was significantly increased in PCOS patients compared to controls in three independent studies employing 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing. Li et al. [20] reported this elevation at all four circadian time points examined (ZT0, ZT6, ZT12 and ZT18). Similarly, Deng et al. [22] and Wang et al. [26] both observed a significant increase in Fusobacterium in PCOS groups. It is noteworthy, however, that at the broader phylum level (Fusobacteriota), the findings were not consistent across studies. While Li et al. [20] reported an increase in the phylum, Wang et al. [26] found no significant difference between the PCOS and control groups. Nonetheless, the consistent observations at the genus level across these studies suggest that an expansion of Fusobacterium may be a feature of the PCOS oral ecosystem. In contrast to the consistent elevation observed at the genus level, findings from the limited studies quantifying Fusobacterium nucleatum (F. nucleatum) were mixed and the observed heterogeneity may be related to variations in periodontal status among the study populations. Akcalı et al. [18] and Joseph et al. [23] reported its elevation, particularly in PCOS subgroups with gingivitis or periodontitis. However, Wendland et al. [21]. found no significant difference in F. nucleatum abundance between the unselected PCOS and control groups.

For other key periodontal pathogens, the evidence was more heterogeneous and appeared to be closely linked to the presence of gingival inflammation in the PCOS participants. Findings regarding Porphyromonas gingivalis (P. gingivalis) were inconsistent. Akcalı et al. [18] reported significantly higher levels in PCOS patients with gingivitis compared to periodontally healthy PCOS individuals, as well as to both systemically and periodontally healthy women and systemically healthy women with gingivitis. In contrast, two other studies [21,23]found no significant difference in P. gingivalis abundance between the unselected PCOS and control groups. Notably, at the genus level, studies also reported opposing trends, with Porphyromonas found to be lower in PCOS patients in one study [26] but higher in another [27]. Similarly, the results for Prevotella intermedia (P. intermedia) and Tannerella forsythia (T. forsythia) were limited and, where reported, showed a pattern dependent on periodontal status. Significant elevations of both species were reported exclusively in the study by Akcalı et al. [18], specifically in the PCOS with gingivitis subgroup compared to healthy controls and periodontally healthy PCOS individuals. Wendland et al. [21], however, detected no significant differences in the abundance of these pathogens between unselected PCOS patients and controls.

Beyond the specific periodontal pathogens, alterations in broader bacterial taxa were also observed, with some phylum-level trends aligning with changes in their constituent genera while others displayed more complex patterns. At the phylum level, the abundance of Firmicutes was consistently lower in women with PCOS compared to healthy controls in two studies [24,26]. Notably, within these same two studies [24,26], the genus Streptococcus, which belongs to the Firmicutes phylum, was also reported to be decreased in the PCOS groups. In contrast, another genus within Firmicutes, Peptococcus, showed opposing results, with one study [22] reporting an increase and another [27] reporting a decrease in its abundance in PCOS. Similarly, the phylum Proteobacteria was found at a higher abundance in PCOS patients in the same two studies [24,26]. However, changes in its constituent genera were not uniform. The genus Neisseria was decreased in one [24] of these studies but showed no significant difference in the other [26]. Furthermore, the periodontal pathogen Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans (A. actinomycetemcomitans), also a member of the Proteobacteria phylum, exhibited no significant difference between PCOS patients and controls in the two studies [18,21] that specifically examined it. Additionally, Actinobacteria abundance was reported to be lower in PCOS patients in two separate studies [19,20].

sec.title: Correlations with periodontal status and systemic inflammation
sec.p: The included studies consistently reported poorer clinical periodontal status in women with PCOS compared to healthy controls, suggesting a potential interplay between PCOS and oral inflammation that warrants further investigation. Specifically, Akcalı et al. reported strong positive correlations between bleeding on probing (BOP) and the salivary counts of multiple periodontal pathogens in PCOS patients, including P. gingivalis (r = 0.549, p < 0.001), T. denticola (r = 0.562, p < 0.001), T. forsythia (r = 0.541, p < 0.001), A. actinomycetemcomitans (r = 0.288, p = 0.010), P. intermedia (r = 0.470, p < 0.001), F. nucleatum (r = 0.404, p < 0.001) and S. oralis (r = 0.305, p = 0.006). These correlations were notably weaker in healthy controls [18]. Similarly, Wendland et al. [21] found significant correlations between the plaque index (PLI) and the counts of red-complex bacteria, orange-complex bacteria, T. denticola and F. nucleatum in adolescents with PCOS (p < 0.05). Extending these findings to more comprehensive clinical measures, Joseph et al. [23] observed significantly higher periodontal inflammatory surface area (PISA) scores in PCOS patients with gingivitis or periodontitis compared to healthy women. Wang et al. [26] also reported that PCOS patients exhibited significantly higher probing depth (PD), bleeding index (BI), clinical attachment loss (AL) and PLI values than healthy controls (p < 0.05). Regarding the systemic immune response, Akcalı et al. [18] found that women with both PCOS and gingivitis had significantly elevated serum antibody levels against P. gingivalis compared to systemically and periodontally healthy women (p = 0.02). Positive correlations were also observed between salivary bacterial counts and their corresponding serum antibody levels for P. gingivalis (r = 0.306, p = 0.001) and F. nucleatum (r = 0.239, p = 0.007) [18]. Collectively, these findings indicate that PCOS is associated with differences in periodontal status and the systemic immune response to oral pathogens.

sec.title: Associations with systemic metabolic and hormonal features of PCOS
sec.p: Beyond local inflammation, alterations in the oral microbiota were significantly correlated with the core systemic features of PCOS, encompassing hormonal dysregulation, insulin resistance and lipid metabolism disturbances. Concerning sex hormones, studies [20,22,27] have consistently reported elevated serum testosterone, luteinizing hormone (LH) and the LH/follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) ratio in PCOS patients compared to controls. These hormonal alterations were directly linked to specific oral microbial shifts. Huang et al. [27] found that the genus Peptococcus was negatively correlated with testosterone and LH levels, while Porphyromonas was positively correlated with LH and the LH/FSH ratio. The same study [27] also reported a positive correlation between Acinetobacter abundance and serum dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate (DHEA-S) levels, and a negative correlation for Gracilibacteria.

Regarding glucose metabolism and insulin resistance, despite some heterogeneity in Homeostatic Model Assessment of Insulin Resistance (HOMA-IR) findings across studies [20,22], specific oral taxa showed significant associations. Further delineating the metabolic profile, oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) data indicated that PCOS patients exhibited increased basal insulin secretion and an upward trend in the area under the insulin curve (AUCinsulin) [20]. Deng et al. [22] reported that the relative abundances of bacterial families such as Spirochaetaceae and genera, including Treponema and Schwartzia, were positively correlated with HOMA-IR and fasting insulin levels. Hilaloğlu et al. [25] focused on oral ecological changes, finding that in insulin-resistant PCOS patients, the salivary count of Streptococcus mutans (S. mutans) was positively correlated with the DMFT index, while the unstimulated salivary flow rate was negatively correlated with both S. mutans count and DMFT index. In terms of lipid metabolism, PCOS patients showed trends of dyslipidemia, such as elevated triglycerides (TG) and reduced high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) [22,27]. These lipid parameters were inversely associated with specific oral microbes. For example, Wendland et al. [21] reported weak negative correlations between HDL-C levels and the counts of A. actinomycetemcomitans (r = −0.39, p = 0.0283) and orange-complex bacteria (r = −0.36, p = 0.0452). Furthermore, Deng et al. [22] noted positive correlations between the abundances of several taxa (e.g. Fusobacterium, Treponema and Capnocytophaga) and various obesity and lipid markers.

Additionally, Li et al. [20] provided functional insights by using metagenomic prediction to observe significant temporal alterations in microbial metabolic pathways (e.g. downregulation of secretion systems and upregulation of oxidative phosphorylation) in PCOS patients across circadian time points. Taken together, these results suggest that the oral microbiome is intricately linked to the multifaceted metabolic and endocrine dysregulation that characterizes PCOS, potentially acting as a modulatory interface or a biomarker of systemic disease activity.

sec.title: Publication bias and sensitivity analyses
sec.p: Publication bias was not formally assessed for the primary outcomes due to the limited number of studies available for each specific endpoint. Subgroup-specific sensitivity analyses were also unfeasible. A leave-one-out sensitivity analysis verified the robustness of the core conclusion that Fusobacterium abundance is significantly elevated in PCOS patients. However, for outcomes involving high heterogeneity, excluding individual studies could alter the interpretation of the overall conclusion.

sec.title: Risk of bias
sec.p: The included studies demonstrated moderate to high methodological quality according to the NOS scale, with scores ranging from 6 to 7 (Table 3). The assessments revealed both strengths and common limitations. A key strength was the consistent control for major confounding factors and the use of identical exposure assessment methods for cases and controls across all studies, which reduces the risk of information bias. However, a notable common limitation was identified in the ‘Selection of Controls’ domain, indicating a potential risk of selection bias.

caption.p: Newcastle-Ottawa Scale for assessing the quality of the included studies.

tr.th: Same method of ascertainment for cases and controls

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Polycystic ovary syndrome is a complex endocrine and metabolic disorder whose etiology has not yet been fully elucidated [28]. While previous research has extensively established the important role of the gut microbiota in the development and progression of PCOS [29], emerging evidence further suggests that the oral microbiota may also contribute to the disease process through pathways such as the oral–gut axis and systemic immune inflammation [1,9]. Through a systematic review, this article synthesizes current clinical and mechanistic evidence on the association between the oral microbiota and PCOS, with the aim of providing new perspectives for a deeper understanding of the role of oral dysbiosis in the pathophysiology of PCOS.

Among the various alterations in the oral microbiota, the aberrant enrichment of periodontal pathogens emerges as the most prominent feature associated with PCOS. In particular, multiple independent 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing studies have consistently confirmed that Fusobacterium represents a core genus with significantly elevated abundance in the PCOS oral ecosystem, a signature that holds true across different experimental settings and circadian time points [20,22,26]. However, consistent conclusions at the species level (specifically regarding F. nucleatum) require further validation, as its enrichment appears to be more closely linked to concomitant periodontal inflammation [18,21,23]. Elevated levels are more likely to be observed in PCOS populations with gingivitis or periodontal disease, whereas no significant differences are found in unselected PCOS populations, highlighting the critical role of the oral local environment in mediating the interplay between this bacterial genus and PCOS. Furthermore, Fusobacterium nucleatum contains four well-characterized subspecies, including Fn subspecies nucleatum (Fnn), Fn subspecies polymorphum (Fnp), Fn subspecies vincentii (Fnv, also referred to as Fn subspecies fusiforme, Fnf) and Fn subspecies animalis (Fna) [30]. The significant intraspecific heterogeneity may confound the observed associations between Fusobacterium nucleatum and PCOS, which represents an important caveat that should be acknowledged when interpreting the current findings. It is within this dysbiotic and inflammatory milieu that Fusobacterium proliferates excessively and may subsequently exert dual impacts on PCOS pathophysiology. On one hand, it can disrupt the integrity of the oral mucosal barrier, facilitate the colonization of other periodontal pathogens, and induce local low-grade inflammation [31,32], which further amplifies the systemic proinflammatory state that is closely linked to PCOS progression [33]. On the other hand, its abundance correlates positively with metabolic abnormalities such as dyslipidemia and obesity [34], forming a positive feedback loop where metabolic disturbances in PCOS patients [35] in turn promote the expansion of Fusobacterium in the oral cavity.

At the level of classical periodontal pathogens, the evidence exhibits marked heterogeneity and context dependency. The association between PCOS and key pathogens, represented by P. gingivalis, P. intermedia and T. forsythia, is not universal but is closely dependent on periodontal clinical status [18,21,23,26,27]. Existing studies indicate that significant proliferation of these bacteria is primarily concentrated in PCOS subgroups already presenting with gingivitis or periodontitis [18], whereas in the broader PCOS population without stratification by periodontal condition, alterations in their abundance are inconsistent or not significantly different [21,23]. This pattern suggests that the potential systemic impact of these keystone pathogens may be conditional upon the presence of active periodontal disease, exacerbating the chronic low-grade inflammatory state inherent to PCOS [36]. Mechanistically, in susceptible individuals with established gingival inflammation, pathogens such as P. gingivalis can induce local tissue destruction, enter the circulation and amplify systemic levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as IL-6 and TNF-α [37,38]. These cytokines are central to the inflammatory pathway of PCOS [39], potentially creating a vicious cycle where systemic inflammation worsens metabolic and endocrine dysfunction [40], which in turn may further compromise oral immune homeostasis. Therefore, the current evidence suggests that the association between specific periodontal pathogens and PCOS may be critically mediated by the local oral inflammatory milieu, highlighting the importance of considering periodontal status as a key variable in future research aimed at elucidating oral‒systemic crosstalk in PCOS (Figure 2).

In line with this context-dependent pattern, alterations in non-pathogenic taxa also appear to be modulated by periodontal health. Two studies [19,20] have observed a consistent reduction in the abundance of Actinobacteria, a phylum containing beneficial microbes [41], in the oral microbiota of women with PCOS. However, this difference diminishes when considering only periodontally healthy individuals, among whom Actinobacteria levels do not significantly differ by PCOS status. This observation strengthens the argument that the depletion of potentially beneficial bacteria, alongside the expansion of pathogens, may primarily reflect a concomitant periodontal disease state rather than constituting an independent feature of PCOS. Nevertheless, once established, this dysbiotic state could engage in complex bidirectional interactions with PCOS-related endocrine and metabolic disturbances via inflammatory pathways, potentially forming a vicious cycle [42].

Beyond these shifts directly linked to periodontal inflammation, broader ecological alterations have been identified in the oral microbiota of PCOS patients, which may represent a more intrinsic dysbiotic signature. The present studies [24,26] revealed a consistent decrease in the abundance of Firmicutes and its constituent genus Streptococcus in the oral microbiota of women with PCOS. Firmicutes is a key component of both oral and gut microbial communities, and alterations in its composition are closely linked to host metabolic status [43]. Streptococcus, as an early colonizer and biofilm former in the oral cavity [44], may reflect overall dysbiosis of the oral microbial ecosystem in PCOS patients when its abundance is reduced. This specific pattern of microbial shift, characterized by a reduction in potentially beneficial or commensal taxa, shares similarities with the microbial features observed in metabolic disorders such as obesity and insulin resistance [45]. Concurrently, the same studies [24,26] reported a significant increase in the abundance of the phylum Proteobacteria in PCOS patients. Proteobacteria encompasses various Gram-negative pathogenic bacteria, and its enrichment is often regarded as a marker of microbial dysbiosis and an inflammatory state [46]. Microorganisms within this phylum can exacerbate local and systemic inflammatory responses through pathways such as lipopolysaccharide (LPS) [47]. Therefore, the observed decrease in Firmicutes and Streptococcus along with the increase in Proteobacteria in the oral cavity of PCOS patients may collectively be associated with the underlying chronic low-grade inflammation and metabolic disturbances in this condition. This provides specific microbiological clues for further investigation into the role of oral microecology in the pathophysiology of PCOS.

In addition to structural dysbiosis of the oral microbiota, the characteristic endocrine disturbances in PCOS may engage in bidirectional interactions with it. P. gingivalis levels are significantly elevated in the oral cavity of PCOS patients and show positive correlations with serum antibody responses and periodontal clinical parameters, such as bleeding on probing [18]. Notably, PCOS is characterized by typical endocrine disturbances, and evidence suggests that such an aberrant hormonal milieu can directly modulate oral microecology [48]. For instance, research by Joseph et al. [23] suggests that PCOS-related changes in sex hormones, potentially via their accumulation in periodontal tissues, may influence the levels of potential periodontal pathogens in saliva as well as the systemic immune response to these pathogens. On the other hand, the oral microbiota may in turn influence sex hormone metabolism or exacerbate insulin resistance, possibly through effects on local and systemic inflammation or via interactions with hormones [4,48]. For example, Porphyromonas showed a positive correlation with the LH/FSH ratio, while Peptococcus was negatively correlated with testosterone and LH levels [28]. This suggests oral microbes might influence hormonal axes through mechanisms such as microbial enzymatic activity, the production of hormone-mimicking molecules, or the modulation of systemic inflammation [37,49]. Notably, the consistent finding of heightened periodontal inflammation and a stronger systemic humoral response to oral pathogens in PCOS patients points to inflammation as a pivotal mechanistic bridge in this interplay. The oral dysbiotic community, particularly keystone pathogens, may perpetuate local inflammation, leading to the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines into the circulation [38]. This systemic inflammation could, in turn, worsen the chronic low-grade inflammation inherent to PCOS, thereby aggravating insulin resistance and further disrupting endocrine homeostasis [50]. Conversely, the hyperandrogenic and pro-inflammatory state of PCOS likely creates a favorable environment for the colonization and pathogenicity of oral bacteria [51,52], thus closing a potential feedback loop. This could collectively aggravate the endocrine and metabolic disturbances observed in PCOS. Therefore, the relationship between oral microorganisms and the endocrine system in PCOS is not unidirectional but rather constitutes a complex bidirectional interaction network. This perspective not only deepens our understanding of PCOS pathophysiology but also meaningfully complements the existing body of research focused on interactions between the gut microbiota and hormones [53,54]. Furthermore, the functional consequences of these microbial shifts, as preliminarily suggested by metagenomic predictions of altered metabolic pathways, require validation through targeted metabolomic and mechanistic investigations [55].

Notably, significant methodological heterogeneity across studies must be considered when interpreting specific microbial changes are interpreted: (1) Differences in sampling sites lead to inconsistent characterization of the oral microbial ecosystem. Recent advances in oral biogeography have confirmed that saliva and subgingival plaque represent fundamentally different ecological niches. Saliva provides a comprehensive view of the overall oral microbial community and is highly sensitive to systemic physiological changes [56], whereas subgingival plaque harbors a more stable, biofilm-structured community and serves as the primary reservoir for keystone periodontal pathogens [57]. For instance, the enrichment of keystone pathogens like P. gingivalis is a hallmark of the subgingival niche [57]. However, two studies using subgingival plaque samples [21,23] did not detect significant differences in the abundance of this pathogen between unselected PCOS populations and control groups. In contrast, Akcalı et al. [18] reported that saliva-based analyses showed significantly higher P. gingivalis abundance in PCOS patients with gingivitis compared to periodontally healthy PCOS individuals and systemically healthy women. This discrepancy in results stems from differences in sampling sites, particularly in PCOS populations without restricted periodontal health status, where subgingival plaque exhibits a stronger dependence on local inflammation. Conversely, the dynamic nature of the salivary microbiome suggests it may serve as a potential sensitive indicator of the characteristic systemic metabolic and endocrine alterations in PCOS, which is consistent with the reported correlations between salivary taxa and clinical parameters in the included studies. Therefore, the discrepancies between saliva- and plaque-based studies can be interpreted as evidence of a site-specific impact of PCOS on the oral ecosystem, but this interpretation warrants caution given the limited number of included studies. The systemic effects of PCOS may first manifest in the more responsive salivary microbiome, whereas alterations in the robust subgingival biofilm may require the additional trigger of local periodontal inflammation, which is more prevalent in PCOS. This perspective underscores that the two sample types are complementary and that their combined analysis facilitates a more comprehensive understanding of oral‒systemic interactions in PCOS. (2) Variations in the targeted regions of the 16S rRNA gene result in distinct discriminatory capacities for different bacterial taxa and can systematically skew the estimated relative abundance of key taxa [58]. For instance, elevated abundance of Actinomyces was reported in a study targeting the V1–V2 region but not in those based on V3–V4 sequencing. Thus, without standardization, regional selection bias can lead to the overestimation or underestimation of microbiota alterations in PCOS patients, directly affecting the comparability and interpretation of findings across studies. (3) Heterogeneity in quantitative PCR (qPCR) assay design, encompassing variations in both the scope of detection targets (ranging from specific pathogens to broad-spectrum panels) and primer strategies (from species-specific to universal primers), may introduce amplification bias [59]. This compromises both the accuracy of microbial quantification and the comparability of results across studies. For instance, studies employing different primers for P. gingivalis have reported inconsistent abundance differences.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This systematic review synthesizes the currently available evidence, but its conclusions must be interpreted within the context of several limitations. First, the literature search was restricted to English and Chinese publications, potentially omitting relevant studies published in other languages, thereby introducing potential publication bias. Second, the number of included studies is relatively limited (n = 10), and some studies themselves had small sample sizes, thereby reducing the statistical power to detect subtle microbial shifts and constraining the generalizability of the findings. However, the included studies, which recruited cohorts from multiple countries or regions and encompassed females spanning adolescence to reproductive age, partially enhance the external validity of the observed associations. A more fundamental constraint lies in the considerable methodological heterogeneity across studies, as detailed above, which collectively limits the accuracy of quantitative analysis and functional inference. However, our quality assessment indicates that the original studies were of moderate to high quality. Although some studies had potential selection bias due to suboptimal control selection, they consistently controlled for key confounding factors and employed standardized exposure assessment methods, which effectively mitigated the aforementioned limitations. However, it is crucial to acknowledge that the current evidence, largely derived from case‒control studies, establishes associations rather than causality.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This systematic review synthesizes existing evidence indicating that oral microbiota alterations in women with PCOS are characterized by a consistently elevated abundance of Fusobacterium and marked heterogeneity in other microbial changes. These analyses imply that such microbial alterations may interact with the host through interconnected pathways related to systemic inflammation, endocrine disturbances and metabolic dysfunction, potentially contributing to the progression and exacerbation of PCOS. Although this systematic review suggests a promising association between the oral microbiota and PCOS that warrants further investigation, the limitations of the included evidence restrict robust causal inference and clinical interpretability. Future studies should therefore prioritize longitudinal designs to elucidate the temporal dynamics and mechanistic pathways linking oral dysbiosis with PCOS progression. In addition, high-quality interventional research is warranted to evaluate whether periodontal therapy or microbiome modulation could serve as novel adjuvant treatments for improving PCOS-related outcomes.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The publication of this review paper was supported by Joint TCM Science & Technology Projects of National Demonstration Zones for Comprehensive TCM Reform (GZY-KJS-ZJ-2026-073). All the authors contributed to editorial changes in the manuscript. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: All data extracted and analyzed during this systematic review are included in the published article and its supplementary information files.

sec.title: Supplemental material
sec.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed at https://doi.org/10.1080/20002297.2026.2650921.
